content.problemStatement:
You are given a **sorted array of unique integers** and a **target number**. Your task is to return the index of the target if it exists in the array. If the target is not present, return the index where it should be inserted so that the array remains sorted.

Your solution must run in **O(log n)** time complexity.

Function DeclarationFunction NamesearchInsertPositionsearchInsertPositionsearchInsertPosition – This function returns the index of a target element in a sorted array. If the element is not present, it returns the position where it should be inserted to maintain sorted order.

Parameters
arrarrarr : A reference to a sorted array of unique integers.
targettargettarget : The integer value to be searched or inserted.
Return Value
Returns the index of targettargettarget if it exists.
Returns the index where targettargettarget should be inserted if it does not exist.
Constraints
1≤T≤101 \leq T \leq 101≤T≤10
1≤n≤1041 \leq n \leq 10^41≤n≤104
−104≤arr[i]≤104-10^4 \leq arr[i] \leq 10^4−104≤arr[i]≤104
Elements in arrarrarr are **unique** and sorted in **non-decreasing order**
−104≤target≤104-10^4 \leq target \leq 10^4−104≤target≤104

Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT — number of test cases.
For each test case:

One line containing two integers: nnn and targettargettarget
One line containing nnn space-separated integers — the sorted array




Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer — the insert position.
constraints: arr is sorted in strictly increasing order
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10) – the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers n and target (1 ≤ n ≤ 10^4, -10^4 ≤ target ≤ 10^4). The second line contains n space‑separated integers – the sorted array of unique values (‑10^4 ≤ arr[i] ≤ 10^4).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the index at which target is found, or the index where it should be inserted to keep the array sorted (0‑based indexing).

sampleTestCases.explanation: First case: target 8 exists at index 2. Second case: 5 is not present; it would be placed between 4 and 8, i.e., index 2. Third case: 12 is larger than all elements, so insertion index is 4.
Target 6 is greater than all array elements; insertion index is 5.

solution.approach: Because the array is sorted and contains unique elements, we can locate the target (or its insertion point) with binary search. Maintain two pointers left and right. While left <= right, compute mid = left + (right-left)/2. If arr[mid] equals target, return mid. If arr[mid] < target, move left to mid+1; otherwise move right to mid-1. When the loop ends, left is the smallest index whose element is greater than target, i.e., the correct insertion position. This runs in O(log n) time and O(1) extra space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(log n) per test case, total O(T·log n).
